13: A shadow: 'Fear God and keep his commandments, for this is the whole duty of man.' Ecclesiastes' closing verdict after surveying all human wisdom is the simplest possible statement of the human vocation: the fear of God and covenant obedience. This is the wisdom tradition's answer to vanity — not a philosophical system but a personal relationship with the Creator. Jesus's summary of the law (love God, love neighbor) is the new covenant's positive restatement of what Ecclesiastes reaches as its final conclusion. But Christ does more than restate: he embodies the fear of God and covenant obedience perfectly (Hebrews 5:7-8: in the days of his flesh, Jesus offered up prayers and supplications with loud cries and tears ... and was heard because of his reverence; although he was a son, he learned obedience through what he suffered), and in his resurrection he breaks the hevel-cycle, proving that labor in the Lord — unlike all labor 'under the sun' — is not in vain (1 Corinthians 15:58).
1: A shadow: remember your Creator in the days of your youth, before the days of trouble come. The 'Creator' here is the unique term bor'echa — the one who formed you — which is the OT ground for worship: your life came from him and returns to him. In the NT this Creator became flesh: 'Through him all things were made' (John 1:3); Christ is the Creator whom Ecclesiastes calls his readers to remember. The urgency — 'before the days of trouble come' — is the NT's 'now is the acceptable time' (2 Corinthians 6:2).
2: A theme: the extended allegory of aging begins — sun, light, moon, and stars grow dark, clouds return after rain. The darkening of the heavens is the image of approaching death and its surrounding sorrows. The NT uses this same cosmic imagery for the crucifixion: 'From the sixth hour there was darkness over all the land until the ninth hour' (Matthew 27:45). What the Preacher uses as an image for individual death, the gospel uses as the sign of the death that undoes death; the sun that went dark on Good Friday rises permanently on Easter.
3: A theme: the body's decay in the aging allegory — trembling keepers, stooped guardians, stilled grinding women, dimmed windows. The NT does not deny this physical dissolution but addresses it through the resurrection body: 'It is sown in dishonor; it is raised in glory. It is sown in weakness; it is raised in power' (1 Corinthians 15:43). The Son of God shared fully in this bodily deterioration (Hebrews 2:14-15: he shared in flesh and blood so that through death he might destroy the power of death), entering the very decay described here in order to reverse it.
4: A theme: the street doors shut, grinding falls silent, one rises at a bird's chirp, voices of song grow dim. The withdrawal from life's sounds and activities continues the aging allegory. Christ entered this silence too: his voice was silenced on the cross, his body buried. But the resurrection overturns this silence permanently: 'Death no longer has dominion over him' (Romans 6:9); the voice that grew dim will be heard at the last trumpet (1 Corinthians 15:52), calling all the silent back to life.
5: A theme: fear of heights, the almond tree's white blossoms (aging), the grasshopper's heaviness (appetite fails), desire fails — the person is going to their bet olamo, their eternal home, while the mourners walk the streets. The phrase 'eternal home' is itself significant: the grave is permanent. But Christ transforms what that 'eternal home' is for those who are his: 'In my Father's house are many rooms ... I go to prepare a place for you' (John 14:2). The mourners walking the streets outside Lazarus's tomb heard a voice that overturned their grief (John 11:43-44).
6: A shadow: before the silver cord is severed, the golden bowl shattered, the pitcher broken at the spring, the wheel at the well — the moment of death's irreversibility. The multiple images of breakage that cannot be undone make death's finality vivid. Christ's death was this breakage: the cord severed, the bowl shattered (the veil torn, the body broken). But the resurrection undoes the irreversibility: the shattered is restored, not to its former state but to glory (John 20:27: the wounds remain, but the person is alive). Breakage in Christ is not final.
7: A direct anticipation: the dust returns to the earth as it was, and the spirit returns to God who gave it. This is Ecclesiastes' clearest statement of the soul's return to its Creator, though it does not specify what that return means. The NT's answer is explicit: Christ 'committed his spirit' into the Father's hands at death (Luke 23:46: Father, into your hands I commit my spirit — citing Psalms 31:5), and the resurrection vindicates that this return is not dissolution but the prelude to bodily restoration. The Preacher describes the pattern; the resurrection demonstrates its outcome.
8: A theme: vapor of vapors — all is vapor. The frame verse that opens the book (Ecclesiastes 1:2) closes it here, placing the whole inquiry within the hevel-bracket. The NT does not contradict this verdict for life 'under the sun' but provides the answer to it: 'Therefore, my beloved brothers, be steadfast, immovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord, knowing that in the Lord your labor is not in vain' (1 Corinthians 15:58). The resurrection is the only event that makes any labor permanently non-vapor; apart from it, Ecclesiastes' verdict stands.
9: A revelation of God: the Teacher was wise and imparted knowledge carefully — listened, investigated, arranged proverbs with precision. This portrait of the faithful teacher who transmits wisdom for others' benefit anticipates the incarnate Teacher. Christ taught 'as one who had authority, and not as their scribes' (Matthew 7:29); he arranged parables and sayings with precision (Matt 13; the Sermon on the Mount); and the Word became flesh so that what was formerly beyond reach became accessible (John 1:14).
10: A revelation of God: the Teacher sought pleasing words, and what he wrote was honest — words of truth. The dual standard — pleasing and true — describes the character of perfect speech, which only Christ fully embodies. Luke 4:22 records both qualities in a single verse: 'all spoke well of him and were amazed at the gracious words that came from his lips.' He is the Truth (John 14:6), and his words accomplish both effects: they are good news and they are exact; they heal and they judge.
11: A type: the words of the wise are like goads and like firmly driven nails — and all come from one Shepherd. The image of the singular Shepherd as the ultimate source of all wisdom is striking and points forward. The NT explicitly identifies Christ as the one Shepherd: 'I am the good shepherd ... there will be one flock, one shepherd' (John 10:11, 14, 16). All true wisdom — from the many teachers and books the verse acknowledges — is derived from the one Shepherd who laid down his life for the sheep; the collection of proverbs converges on a person.
12: A theme: the endless proliferation of books and the exhaustion of much study. The Preacher places all human learning in proper perspective: the accumulation of texts does not yield life. The NT offers what the many books cannot: the Word himself (John 1:14); 'these are written so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that by believing you may have life in his name' (John 20:31). The purpose of writing converges in the gospel; what exhausts in books finds rest in a person.
14: A direct anticipation: God will bring every act into judgment — every hidden thing, whether good or evil. This is the clearest eschatological declaration in Ecclesiastes, and the NT fulfills it with specificity: God has fixed the day of judgment and appointed the judge — the risen Christ (Acts 17:31). Paul's 'we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ' (2 Corinthians 5:10) is the direct NT statement. The 'hidden things' — the secret acts and the inner motives Ecclesiastes could only gesture toward — will be brought to light (1 Corinthians 4:5: he will bring to light what is hidden in darkness and will expose the motives of the heart).
